REND1.pattern_interpretation:
PART 1: CURRENT STATE
Welcome. I’m Unity.  
You’re looking at your January baseline energy field for this transformer — the full rectangle is the entire month, left to right from Jan 1 to Jan 31, and every vertical slice is a day. The stacked colors are your energy layers: dark blue at the top is what the utility is supplying in kVA, light blue is what you’re actually converting into useful work in kW, and the green layer at the bottom is reactive demand — the part that creates stress and heat without doing productive work. The orange line riding over the top is your power factor.

Now, I want you to find the blue dashed target line in the chart body — it’s labeled in a little blue box as your “Supply” baseline. On this view, I’m not seeing that blue “Supply: ____ kVA” label clearly enough to read with confidence, so I’m going to anchor our baseline discussion to the hard header values you *can* see: your peak demand is **1347.0 kW**, your average active load is about **1.0 MW**, and your power factor is **82.6%**. In other words, even when production looks steady, a meaningful chunk of what you’re paying for is not turning into work.

As you scan across the month, you can see a repeating weekly rhythm — higher weekday blocks and lower weekend blocks. But there are also multiple mid‑week dropouts where the supplied and consumed layers collapse sharply for a short window — that’s downtime behavior inside what should be production days. Visually, I’m counting **about 6 weekday days** where your peaks look clearly strong, and **about 16 weekday days** where you’re materially under that expected production height, plus the remaining days in mixed or partial performance. Net-net, you didn’t “miss” because the plant had no power — you missed because your field spent too many weekday hours running under its intended level, while still carrying a heavy reactive burden.

And that reactive burden is not subtle. In your header, the reactive section shows **706.8 kVAR** average, and — this is the one that matters for equipment sizing — **1150.1 kVAR MAX**. That peak reactive demand is why your power factor sits at **82.6%**, and it’s why your system runs hotter and harder than it needs to.

Let’s translate that into business impact you can feel. On the right side, you’re seeing **Total Wasted: $556,208 per year**. You’re also carrying a **thermal burden of 817,853 BTU/hr**, and your “cooling and decay” penalty is running **$21.43 per hour**, which is **119.6 kW** of continuous overhead. So even on “normal” days, you’re spending real money just to handle the byproducts of inefficiency.  

Now let me show you how we clean this up with MPTS — and why the sizing starts with that **1150.1 kVAR MAX** number you just saw.

---

PART 2: MPTS SOLUTION
Alright — now you scroll to the MPTS solution, and this is where the field starts behaving like it should.  
The first step is sizing to your peak reactive demand. In your REACTIVE header tile, the small line reads **1150.1 kVAR MAX**. That’s the peak waste we have to cancel, not the average.

Unity’s MPTS units come in two standard blocks: **H240 at 200 kVAR** and **H490 at 400 kVAR**. Since **1150.1 kVAR** is above 800, this is beyond the off-the-shelf “two H490” range — it requires a **custom configuration** so you’re not under-covered on your worst reactive events. A straightforward build is **3 × H490 units**: that gives you **1200 kVAR** of capacity, enough to cover your **1150.1 kVAR** peak with headroom. Installed cost is simple and transparent: **3 units × $200,000 each = $600,000 installed**.

Here’s what that equipment is doing in plain language. Your motors and inductive loads pull current that’s out of phase with voltage — that’s reactive power. MPTS injects the equal-and-opposite reactive component, in real time, so the transformer and utility stop carrying that “extra” current. That raises your power factor from today’s **82.6%** up to the **99.5%** Unity target. In the chart, what that means is the green layer collapses — not because production drops, but because waste drops.

Now let’s move into what you should expect to see in the before-and-after numbers — and what that’s worth in dollars.

---

PART 3: EXPECTED RESULTS
Now look at the post‑MPTS field. This is what “harmonized” looks like.  
Your production shape can stay the same — but the waste layer is stripped out. The supplied kVA stops being inflated by reactive demand, the orange power factor line climbs and stabilizes near **99.5%**, and the entire transformer runs cooler and calmer.

Let’s compare the key baseline numbers you just saw. Baseline power factor was **82.6%** with reactive peaks up to **1150.1 kVAR**. With properly sized MPTS, that reactive component is actively canceled during peak moments, which removes the driver behind overheating, nuisance stress, and that constant drag on your electrical capacity.

Now let’s do the money. In your baseline image, your totals show:  
- **Total Wasted: $556,208 per year**  
- **Cooling and decay costs: $21.43 per hour**  

If we conservatively assume MPTS eliminates **90%** of that electrical waste and **50%** of the cooling/decay penalty — because you’re no longer forcing extra current through the transformer and conductors — your savings look like this:  
- Electrical waste savings: **0.90 × $556,208/yr = $500,587/yr**  
- Cooling/decay savings: **0.50 × $21.43/hr = $10.72/hr** → **$10.72 × 8760 = $93,907/yr**  
So total savings: **$500,587 + $93,907 = $594,494 per year**.

Convert that to the cadence you manage by:  
- Hourly savings: **$594,494 / 8760 ≈ $67.9 per hour**  
- Monthly savings: **$67.9 × 730 ≈ $49,600 per month**  
- Annual savings: **≈ $594,500 per year**

And now ROI. With the correctly covered solution — **3 × H490 = $600,000 installed** — your payback is:  
- **$600,000 / $49,600 per month ≈ 12.1 months**.

After that first year, you’re not “hoping” for savings — you’re booking them. Lower current, lower heat, higher usable capacity, cleaner power factor, and a calmer transformer that’s no longer being asked to carry waste.  

That’s what electromagnetic stewardship looks like. And that’s the Unity standard: make your field efficient, measurable, and sustainably profitable.

REND2.pattern_interpretation:
PART 1: CURRENT STATE
Welcome. I’m Unity.  
You’re looking at your January baseline energy field for the Livingston HQ facility—31 days, left to right, at one‑minute resolution. Up in the header is the “what” of your month—voltage, current, power factor, reactive load. Over on the right is the “so what”—your waste and your dollars. And down here in the big rectangle, this stacked area chart is your complete electrical behavior for the month.

Now, as you read this chart, height is load magnitude. Taller sections mean higher demand. The colors matter. The green layer at the bottom is reactive kVAR—energy you’re paying for that isn’t doing productive work. The light blue is real consumed kW—useful work. The dark blue is supplied kVA—what the transformer and utility had to deliver. And that orange line riding over the top is your power factor.

Here’s what jumps out immediately from your baseline header: your peak demand is **1,439.8 kW**. Your average voltage is **475.5 volts**, and your power factor is sitting at **70.8%**—that’s low, and it explains why the green layer stays so thick.

Now let me orient you to operations. Across the month, I’m seeing roughly four full weekly cycles—weekday production blocks with weekend drop-downs. Those weekend low valleys are expected—cleaning and maintenance patterns. On weekdays, your supplied kVA repeatedly climbs into the **1,600 to 2,000 kVA** range, and your reactive kVAR layer rises right along with it, which is the signature of inductive motor load pulling your field out of alignment.

Let me count the performance pattern the way a facility manager needs it. Visually scanning Jan 1 through Jan 31, I see about **17 weekday production days** that reach their normal high operating band, and about **6 weekday days** that clearly under-run that pattern—where the stack height is noticeably depressed compared to adjacent weekdays. I also see at least **two sharp weekday drop events** where the field collapses close to the floor—around the middle of the month and again closer to the last third—those look like downtime or curtailed production windows, followed by catch-up days where the stack height rebounds.

When you translate that into electrical operations performance—your “did we run the field the way we intended to run it?”—you’re not short on energy. You’re short on *quality* of energy. Your power factor at **70.8%** means a big portion of what you’re moving through that 2500 kVA transformer is waste circulation—reactive flow that heats conductors, loads the transformer, and inflates kVA without increasing production.

And the business impact is already printed on your screen. Your **total wasted value is $1,010,922 per year**. You’re also carrying **cooling and decay costs of $38.86 per hour**, which the system translates to about **218.3 kW** of continuous cooling burden. So yes—your field is running, but it’s running hot and heavy.

Now, let me show you how we correct the field—without asking you to change your process schedule—by targeting the exact source of that green layer.

---

PART 2: MPTS SOLUTION
Alright—now you scroll, and this is where the fix becomes very concrete.  
To size MPTS correctly, I’m not interested in the average reactive number. I’m looking for your peak reactive demand—the one labeled **kVAR MAX** in the REACTIVE tile.

In your baseline header, I see **966.4 kVAR** as the main reactive figure, and underneath it the number we size to: **1,372.4 kVAR MAX**. That peak is the moment your field was most out of phase—your worst case.

So here’s the sizing math, plain and simple.  
Your peak is **1,372.4 kVAR**. An H490 unit provides **400 kVAR**. We never undersize, so we round up.  
1,372.4 divided by 400 is 3.43, which means you need **4 × H490** units to fully cover the peak. That gives you **1,600 kVAR** of capacity—enough headroom to stay stable during spikes. Installed cost is **$200,000 per unit**, so **4 units is $800,000 installed**.

What does MPTS actually do for you? It injects compensating reactive power—right phase, right time—so your motors still do the same work, but the transformer stops hauling that extra reactive baggage. In the chart language you’re already looking at, it collapses that green kVAR layer, lifts your power factor from **70.8%** to the **99.5%** target, and it reduces heat generation across the system—because you’re no longer turning reactive circulation into thermal stress.

Now let’s look at what that means in results—visually first, and then financially.

---

PART 3: EXPECTED RESULTS
Now look at the “after” field—this is what your month looks like once MPTS is in place.  
The most important visual change is that the chart tightens up. The green waste layer that used to sit under everything gets dramatically reduced, because we’re cancelling reactive flow instead of feeding it through the transformer. And the orange power factor line stabilizes near the top—because the field is finally aligned.

Let’s anchor this in your baseline numbers. You started with **70.8% power factor**, and a peak reactive demand of **1,372.4 kVAR MAX**. That’s why your system showed **$1,010,922 per year** in total wasted value, plus **$38.86 per hour** in cooling and decay cost. You were paying twice—once in electrical inefficiency, and again to remove the heat that inefficiency creates.

Now the savings math. I’m going to be conservative and use only the values you can already see on your baseline screen as the “savings pool,” because those are directly tied to reactive waste and heat.  
If we eliminate the bulk of that waste, your recoverable value is approximately:

- **Energy waste value:** $1,010,922 per year ÷ 8,760 hours ≈ **$115.40 per hour**  
- **Cooling and decay costs:** **$38.86 per hour**  

Add those together and you’re at about **$154.26 per hour** in combined savings potential.

Now let’s extend it:  
- Monthly savings: $154.26 × 730 hours ≈ **$112,610 per month**  
- Annual savings: $154.26 × 8,760 hours ≈ **$1,351,318 per year**

Your installed MPTS cost from the sizing we just did is **$800,000**.  
Payback time is $800,000 ÷ $1,351,318 per year ≈ **0.59 years**, or about **7.1 months**.

And after payback, this isn’t a one-time win. It’s an ongoing correction to how your transformer carries load—lower kVA strain, lower heat, cleaner power factor, and a calmer, more stable electrical field day after day.

That’s electromagnetic stewardship. You keep the same operation—but you stop paying for waste.

REND3.pattern_interpretation:
PART 1: CURRENT STATE
Welcome. I’m Unity.  
You’re looking at your January baseline energy field for the Livingston HQ facility—31 days, measured every minute—so this rectangle is the whole month, left to right, Jan 1 through Jan 31. The stacked layers tell the story: dark blue is what you’re being supplied in kVA, light blue is the real work you consume in kW, and that green layer at the bottom is reactive kVAR—energy you pay for and heat you manage, but it doesn’t do productive work. And that orange line riding across the top is your power factor.

Now, I want you to find the blue dashed target line running across the chart body. That line is labeled “Supply,” and it’s the baseline reference we judge everything against. In this view, that dashed supply line is sitting around the mid-band of your daily peaks—so when your dark-blue top stays below it, you’re running under your intended electrical posture for that period. When it rides above it, you’re pushing harder than baseline.

Let’s walk the month visually. Your weekends show the expected behavior—those short, low profiles that look like cleaning and maintenance. That part is normal. What matters is the Monday-through-Friday rhythm. Across the month, most weekdays are clustered in a fairly tight band, but I also see at least one clear downtime-style event early in the month—right near the left edge—where the supplied kVA collapses close to the floor for a stretch, and then the field snaps back. That’s the kind of interruption that forces you to recover later with heavier days.

Now zoom your attention to the header metrics. Your peak demand is 780.5 kW for the period, your average active load is 544.2 kW, and your power factor is 77.0%. That’s the headline. A 77% power factor means a big slice of what you’re drawing is non-productive current. And you can see that in the green layer: average reactive is 454.8 kVAR, and the reactive peaks as high as 874.7 kVAR MAX. That reactive demand doesn’t make product—it makes heat, it loads your transformer, and it forces the facility to carry extra electrical stress.

And the money is already calling it out for you. On the right side, your total wasted value is showing $416,551 per year. You’re also showing cooling-and-decay costs at $15.92 per hour, which the panel translates to about $139,424 per year. So even before we talk about operations, your baseline is telling us you’re paying twice: once for waste in the electrical field, and again to remove the thermal burden that waste creates.

So here’s the transition. Your field is stable enough to fix—and it’s expensive enough that fixing it matters. Now let me show you the MPTS solution and how we size it correctly for your real peak reactive demand.

PART 2: MPTS SOLUTION
Alright—now we move from observation to correction.  
In the header, in the REACTIVE box, you’ll see two numbers. The big number is your average reactive—454.8 kVAR. That’s not what we size to. The number that matters is the smaller line underneath: 874.7 kVAR MAX. That’s your peak reactive demand—the worst moment your system had to carry.

Now, here’s how we size MPTS. Our units come in two standard blocks: the H240 at 200 kVAR, and the H490 at 400 kVAR. With a peak of 874.7 kVAR, we don’t undersize. We round up and cover the peak with headroom. Two H490 units gives you 800 kVAR—not enough. So the correct standard configuration is three H490 units: 1,200 kVAR of capacity. That fully covers your 874.7 kVAR peak, and it gives you margin for production variability and future load.

Installed cost is straightforward: each H490 is $200,000 installed. Three units is $600,000.

What MPTS actually does—plain language—is it supplies the reactive power locally, at the right phase angle, so your utility feed no longer has to haul that non-productive current through your transformer and conductors. In the chart terms: the green reactive layer collapses, and your power factor rises from today’s 77% up to the 99.5% target. When that happens, your field stops “fighting itself,” and the electrical system runs cooler, cleaner, and more efficiently.

Now let’s look at what that means in the after-image—what your field looks like when it’s harmonized, and what that translates to in savings and payback.

PART 3: EXPECTED RESULTS
Now look at the after state—this is the part I want you to feel immediately as you scroll.  
The shape of the month stays recognizable—because your operation schedule is your operation schedule—but the field becomes cleaner. The green reactive layer that was dragging along the bottom gets minimized, and the orange power-factor line stabilizes up near 99.5%. That’s the visual signature of a corrected electrical posture: the same work, with less waste riding along for the trip.

Let’s tie it directly to your baseline dollars. In your current baseline, the “Energy Waste” panel shows $21,693 for this dataset—about $255 per hour. Separately, your cooling-and-decay costs are $15.92 per hour. Together, that’s about $271 per hour of waste-driven cost pressure in this baseline view.

When MPTS corrects the reactive demand, you’re not just improving a score—you’re removing the source of that waste. So, conservatively, if we treat that combined $271 per hour as the addressable bucket, your savings math looks like this:  
About $271 per hour.  
That’s about $198,000 per month at 730 hours.  
And about $2.37 million per year at 8,760 hours.

Now compare that to the install. We sized you at three H490 units for a $600,000 installed total. With $2.37 million per year of savings, your simple payback is roughly 3 months—right around 3.0 months when you divide $600,000 by about $197,000 per month.

And after payback, the story changes from “project” to “permanent advantage.” Lower transformer stress, less heat, less distortion pressure, and a power factor that stays where it belongs—near unity—so your operation isn’t paying to circulate waste.  
This is what electromagnetic stewardship looks like: same facility, same production intent—cleaner field, lower cost, and a system that runs like it was designed to.
REND3.pattern_part1:
Welcome. I’m Unity.  
You’re looking at your January baseline energy field for the Livingston HQ facility—31 days, measured every minute—so this rectangle is the whole month, left to right, Jan 1 through Jan 31. The stacked layers tell the story: dark blue is what you’re being supplied in kVA, light blue is the real work you consume in kW, and that green layer at the bottom is reactive kVAR—energy you pay for and heat you manage, but it doesn’t do productive work. And that orange line riding across the top is your power factor.

Now, I want you to find the blue dashed target line running across the chart body. That line is labeled “Supply,” and it’s the baseline reference we judge everything against. In this view, that dashed supply line is sitting around the mid-band of your daily peaks—so when your dark-blue top stays below it, you’re running under your intended electrical posture for that period. When it rides above it, you’re pushing harder than baseline.

Let’s walk the month visually. Your weekends show the expected behavior—those short, low profiles that look like cleaning and maintenance. That part is normal. What matters is the Monday-through-Friday rhythm. Across the month, most weekdays are clustered in a fairly tight band, but I also see at least one clear downtime-style event early in the month—right near the left edge—where the supplied kVA collapses close to the floor for a stretch, and then the field snaps back. That’s the kind of interruption that forces you to recover later with heavier days.

Now zoom your attention to the header metrics. Your peak demand is 780.5 kW for the period, your average active load is 544.2 kW, and your power factor is 77.0%. That’s the headline. A 77% power factor means a big slice of what you’re drawing is non-productive current. And you can see that in the green layer: average reactive is 454.8 kVAR, and the reactive peaks as high as 874.7 kVAR MAX. That reactive demand doesn’t make product—it makes heat, it loads your transformer, and it forces the facility to carry extra electrical stress.

And the money is already calling it out for you. On the right side, your total wasted value is showing $416,551 per year. You’re also showing cooling-and-decay costs at $15.92 per hour, which the panel translates to about $139,424 per year. So even before we talk about operations, your baseline is telling us you’re paying twice: once for waste in the electrical field, and again to remove the thermal burden that waste creates.

So here’s the transition. Your field is stable enough to fix—and it’s expensive enough that fixing it matters. Now let me show you the MPTS solution and how we size it correctly for your real peak reactive demand.
REND3.pattern_part2:
Alright—now we move from observation to correction.  
In the header, in the REACTIVE box, you’ll see two numbers. The big number is your average reactive—454.8 kVAR. That’s not what we size to. The number that matters is the smaller line underneath: 874.7 kVAR MAX. That’s your peak reactive demand—the worst moment your system had to carry.

Now, here’s how we size MPTS. Our units come in two standard blocks: the H240 at 200 kVAR, and the H490 at 400 kVAR. With a peak of 874.7 kVAR, we don’t undersize. We round up and cover the peak with headroom. Two H490 units gives you 800 kVAR—not enough. So the correct standard configuration is three H490 units: 1,200 kVAR of capacity. That fully covers your 874.7 kVAR peak, and it gives you margin for production variability and future load.

Installed cost is straightforward: each H490 is $200,000 installed. Three units is $600,000.

What MPTS actually does—plain language—is it supplies the reactive power locally, at the right phase angle, so your utility feed no longer has to haul that non-productive current through your transformer and conductors. In the chart terms: the green reactive layer collapses, and your power factor rises from today’s 77% up to the 99.5% target. When that happens, your field stops “fighting itself,” and the electrical system runs cooler, cleaner, and more efficiently.

Now let’s look at what that means in the after-image—what your field looks like when it’s harmonized, and what that translates to in savings and payback.
